nodes.label: Boylan & Syntetos: Intermittent Demand Forecasting (Context, Methods and Applications)
nodes.author: John E. Boylan; Aris A. Syntetos
nodes.rationale: 2021 Wiley monograph on forecasting and stock control for intermittent (sporadic, lumpy) demand items.
nodes.norm_label: boylan & syntetos: intermittent demand forecasting (context, methods and applications)

nodes.label: Box & Jenkins: Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control
nodes.author: George Box; Gwilym Jenkins
nodes.rationale: Foundational text on ARIMA modelling and the iterative identification-estimation-checking methodology for time series forecasting and control. NOTE: supplied text file was entirely OCR watermark with no extractable body content; node retained as a citable source only.
nodes.norm_label: box & jenkins: time series analysis: forecasting and control

nodes.label: Gilliland et al.: Business Forecasting: Practical Problems and Solutions
nodes.author: Michael Gilliland (ed.); Len Tashman; Udo Sglavo
nodes.rationale: Practitioner-oriented anthology covering the process, politics, and practical pitfalls of business demand forecasting.
nodes.norm_label: gilliland et al.: business forecasting: practical problems and solutions

nodes.label: Intermittent Demand
nodes.rationale: Demand that occurs sporadically with many zero-demand periods; dominates after-sales/spare-parts inventories and is hard to forecast with standard methods.

nodes.label: Lumpy Demand
nodes.rationale: Intermittent demand where, when demand occurs, the size is highly variable; the hardest pattern to forecast (sporadic + variable size).

nodes.label: Erratic Demand
nodes.rationale: Demand with highly variable size but regular timing; a category distinct from lumpy/intermittent in demand classification schemes.

nodes.label: Demand Classification / Categorisation
nodes.rationale: Segmenting SKUs (e.g. smooth/erratic/intermittent/lumpy) so the appropriate forecasting method can be assigned per category.

nodes.label: Average Inter-Demand Interval (ADI)
nodes.rationale: Mean number of periods between demand occurrences; a primary axis (with CV-squared) for classifying intermittence and choosing methods.

nodes.label: Squared Coefficient of Variation of Demand Size (CV^2)
nodes.rationale: Measures variability of non-zero demand sizes; combined with ADI to draw cut-off boundaries between demand categories (SBC classification).
nodes.norm_label: squared coefficient of variation of demand size (cv^2)

nodes.label: Syntetos-Boylan-Croston (SBC) Classification
nodes.rationale: Classification scheme using ADI and CV^2 cut-offs to decide whether SES, Croston, or SBA should be applied to a series.

nodes.label: Single (Simple) Exponential Smoothing (SES)
nodes.rationale: Baseline smoothing of all periods including zeros; biased upward after demand-occurring periods for intermittent series, motivating Croston's method.

nodes.label: Croston's Method
nodes.rationale: Decomposes demand into separately smoothed demand SIZE and demand INTERVAL to overcome SES bias; collapses to SES when demand is not intermittent.

nodes.label: Size-Interval Decomposition
nodes.rationale: Core idea of Croston-family methods: forecast the mean demand size and the mean inter-demand interval independently, then combine as size/interval.

nodes.label: Croston Inversion Bias
nodes.rationale: Croston's estimator of demand per period is biased because the expectation of a ratio is not the ratio of expectations; quantified and corrected by SBA.

nodes.label: Syntetos-Boylan Approximation (SBA)
nodes.rationale: Applies a correction factor to Croston's method to remove inversion bias; performs well empirically and is simple to implement in practice.

nodes.label: Bias Correction Factor Framework
nodes.rationale: General framework for deriving multiplicative correction factors that debias size-interval estimators such as Croston's.

nodes.label: Teunter-Syntetos-Babai (TSB) Method
nodes.rationale: Croston variant that updates demand probability every period (not just on occurrence), designed to handle obsolescence and declining demand.

nodes.label: Hyperbolic Exponential Smoothing (HES)
nodes.rationale: Smoothing variant developed for intermittent demand to address bias issues in size-interval estimation.

nodes.label: Moving Average
nodes.rationale: Simple average of previous observations used as a baseline forecast; compared against SES and Croston for intermittent series.

nodes.label: Temporal Aggregation
nodes.rationale: Summing demand into longer time buckets reduces intermittence and zero-counts, often improving forecast accuracy before disaggregation.

nodes.label: Cross-Sectional Aggregation
nodes.rationale: Aggregating across related SKUs/locations to pool sparse demand signals and stabilise estimates.

nodes.label: Aggregate-Disaggregate Intermittent Demand Approach (ADIDA)
nodes.rationale: Temporal-aggregation framework: aggregate the series, forecast at the higher level, then disaggregate back to the original frequency.

nodes.label: Temporal Hierarchy Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Reconciling forecasts produced at multiple temporal aggregation levels; Kourentzes & Athanasopoulos showed accuracy gains combined with TSB/ETS.

nodes.label: Lead-Time Demand (LTD)
nodes.rationale: Total demand over the replenishment lead time (plus review interval); the quantity whose distribution drives safety-stock and order-up-to levels.

nodes.label: Lead-Time Demand Distribution
nodes.rationale: Forecasting the whole distribution (not just the mean) of LTD is required because inventory targets depend on its upper quantiles.

nodes.label: Poisson Distribution
nodes.rationale: Common count model for the number of demand occurrences; building block for compound demand models of intermittent series.

nodes.label: Negative Binomial Distribution (NBD)
nodes.rationale: Overdispersed count distribution widely used to model intermittent/lumpy lead-time demand where variance exceeds the mean.

nodes.label: Compound Demand Distribution
nodes.rationale: Models total demand as a random number of demand occurrences (e.g. Poisson) each of a random size; natural fit for intermittent demand.

nodes.label: Integer Autoregressive (INAR/INARMA) Models
nodes.rationale: Count-data time-series models (integer-valued AR/ARMA) that respect the discrete, non-negative nature of intermittent demand.

nodes.label: Bootstrapping for Intermittent Demand
nodes.rationale: Resampling demand sizes and intervals to build an empirical lead-time-demand distribution without assuming a parametric form.

nodes.label: Willemain Bootstrap (Markov-Chain Bootstrap)
nodes.rationale: Bootstrap variant using a Markov chain for occurrence and jittering of sizes to estimate intermittent lead-time-demand distributions.

nodes.label: Overlapping vs Non-Overlapping Blocks
nodes.rationale: Block-resampling design choice that trades off sample reuse against independence when bootstrapping demand series.

nodes.label: Order-Up-To (R,S) Policy
nodes.rationale: Periodic-review inventory policy raising stock to level S every R periods; modified (R,S) policies perform well for intermittent items.

nodes.label: Continuous Review Inventory Control
nodes.rationale: Reviewing/updating stock only when a transaction occurs incurs little cost for intermittent items and depends on lead-time demand only.

nodes.label: Safety Stock
nodes.rationale: Buffer inventory derived from the lead-time-demand distribution and target service level to absorb demand and supply variability.

nodes.label: Service Level (Cycle Service Level / Fill Rate)
nodes.rationale: Inventory performance targets (CSL, CSL+, fill rate) that translate the LTD distribution into stock-holding decisions.
nodes.norm_label: service level (cycle service level / fill rate)

nodes.label: Forecast Error
nodes.rationale: Difference between forecast and actual; basis for all accuracy and bias metrics, but problematic for intermittent series with many zeros.

nodes.label: Mean Square Error (MSE / RMSE)
nodes.rationale: Scale-dependent squared-error accuracy measure; MMSE is the optimality criterion for many forecasting estimators.

nodes.label: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
nodes.rationale: Scale-dependent absolute-error measure; for intermittent demand a forecast of zero can minimise MAE, which motivates scaled/relative measures.

nodes.label: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)
nodes.rationale: Percentage accuracy measure that breaks down for intermittent demand because actuals of zero make percentage errors undefined/infinite.

nodes.label: Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE)
nodes.rationale: Scales MAE by the in-sample error of a naive benchmark, giving a scale-free measure robust to zeros; preferred for intermittent demand.

nodes.label: Mean Error / Forecast Bias
nodes.rationale: Average signed error measuring systematic over/under-forecasting; unbiasedness is a key desideratum, central to the Croston/SBA debate.

nodes.label: Probability Integral Transform (PIT)
nodes.rationale: Distributional-forecast evaluation tool (and randomised rPIT for counts) checking whether the predicted LTD distribution is well calibrated.

nodes.label: Brier Score
nodes.rationale: Proper scoring rule for probabilistic forecasts of demand occurrence (whether demand happens in a period).

nodes.label: Forecast Support System (FSS)
nodes.rationale: Software environment that delivers statistical forecasts and supports human judgement, sitting inside ERP/MRP planning processes.

nodes.label: Forecast Value Added (FVA)
nodes.rationale: Compares each process step against a naive benchmark to identify and eliminate activities that do not improve (or even worsen) the forecast.

nodes.label: Naive Forecast (Benchmark)
nodes.rationale: Random-walk/last-value benchmark; any method or process step that cannot beat it is not adding value (basis of FVA).

nodes.label: Forecastability
nodes.rationale: The inherent limit on achievable accuracy set by the nature of the series; recognising it shifts effort from chasing accuracy to improving process efficiency.

nodes.label: Forecast Benchmarking
nodes.rationale: Comparing forecasting performance against naive baselines and peers to set realistic performance objectives.

nodes.label: Forecasting Process
nodes.rationale: The organisational workflow (statistical baseline -> judgemental adjustment -> consensus) whose efficiency, not just accuracy, should be managed.

nodes.label: Judgmental Adjustment / Override
nodes.rationale: Human edits to statistical forecasts; often destroy value, but trust (and smaller harmful adjustments) rises when uncertainty is communicated in plain language.

nodes.label: Tracking Signal
nodes.rationale: Monitors sustained divergence between forecast and actuals so persistent bias is flagged and corrected quickly.

nodes.label: Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)
nodes.rationale: Cross-functional consensus process aligning demand forecasts with supply and financial plans.

nodes.label: New Product Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Forecasting items with no history; subject to documented 'worst practices' and best addressed with launch dashboards and structured tracking.

nodes.label: Politics of Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Organisational incentives and gaming that distort forecasts; a major non-technical barrier to forecast quality.

nodes.label: Communicating Forecast Uncertainty
nodes.rationale: Expressing prediction intervals in everyday language ('worst-case'/'best-case') increases user trust and reduces harmful overrides.

nodes.label: Machine Learning in Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Big-data/ML/data-science methods attract attention but do not replace the art and science of classical forecasting practice.

nodes.label: Demand Planning
nodes.rationale: The end-to-end function of generating and managing demand forecasts to drive supply-chain and S&OP decisions.

nodes.label: Focus on Process Efficiency, Not Just Accuracy
nodes.rationale: Because forecastability caps achievable accuracy, organisations should accept that limit and instead remove wasteful process steps and pursue non-forecasting solutions.
nodes.norm_label: focus on process efficiency, not just accuracy

nodes.label: Decompose Demand to Remove Smoothing Bias
nodes.rationale: SES is biased on intermittent series after demand-occurring periods, so demand is split into size and interval (Croston) and then bias-corrected (SBA).
nodes.norm_label: decompose demand to remove smoothing bias

nodes.label: Forecast the Whole LTD Distribution for Stock Control
nodes.rationale: Inventory targets depend on upper quantiles of lead-time demand, so a point forecast of the mean is insufficient; the full distribution (parametric or bootstrapped) is required.
nodes.norm_label: forecast the whole ltd distribution for stock control

nodes.label: Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed)
nodes.author: Rob J. Hyndman and George Athanasopoulos

nodes.label: tsibble (Time Series Tibble)
nodes.rationale: A tidy data structure for time series requiring a unique time index per combination of key variables; enables many series to be stored and modelled together.

nodes.label: Key Variables
nodes.rationale: Identify individual time series within a collection; a unique index per key combination makes the tsibble valid.

nodes.label: Seasonal Period
nodes.rationale: The number of observations before the seasonal pattern repeats; many models only support integer seasonal periods, so approximating to integers is useful.

nodes.label: Multiple Seasonal Periods
nodes.rationale: Data observed more than once per week often has more than one seasonal pattern (e.g. daily data with weekly and annual seasonality).

nodes.label: Time Plot
nodes.rationale: The obvious first graph: observations plotted against time, joined by lines, to reveal trend, seasonality, anomalies and structural features that any model must account for.

nodes.label: Trend
nodes.rationale: A long-term increase or decrease in the data; does not have to be linear and can change direction.

nodes.label: Seasonality
nodes.rationale: A pattern of fixed and known period driven by calendar factors (time of year, day of week); distinct from cyclic behaviour.

nodes.label: Cyclic Behaviour
nodes.rationale: Rises and falls not of a fixed frequency, usually tied to economic/business cycles; longer and more variable in magnitude than seasonal patterns.

nodes.label: Seasonal Plot
nodes.rationale: Data plotted against the individual seasons so each year overlaps, making the underlying seasonal pattern and years where it changes easier to see.

nodes.label: Seasonal Subseries Plot
nodes.rationale: Collects data for each season into separate mini time plots with means, revealing changes within particular seasons over time.

nodes.label: Scatterplot
nodes.rationale: Plots one series against another to visualise relationships between variables (e.g. electricity demand vs temperature).

nodes.label: Correlation Coefficient
nodes.rationale: Measures the strength of the LINEAR relationship between two variables; can be misleading for non-linear relationships, so always inspect the plots.

nodes.label: Scatterplot Matrix
nodes.rationale: Plots every variable against every other, giving a quick view of all pairwise relationships when several potential predictors exist.

nodes.label: Lag Plot
nodes.rationale: Plots y_t against lagged values y_{t-k} to reveal seasonality (strong positive relationship at seasonal lags).

nodes.label: Autocorrelation
nodes.rationale: Measures the linear relationship between lagged values of a single time series; r_k is the correlation between y_t and y_{t-k}.

nodes.label: Autocorrelation Function (ACF)
nodes.rationale: The set of autocorrelation coefficients plotted against lag (a correlogram); trend shows slow decay, seasonality shows larger values at seasonal lags.

nodes.label: Correlogram
nodes.rationale: The graphical plot of the ACF against lag.

nodes.label: White Noise
nodes.rationale: Time series showing no autocorrelation; 95% of ACF spikes should lie within +/- 1.96/sqrt(T), used to test whether a series is unpredictable.

nodes.label: Time Series Decomposition
nodes.rationale: Splitting a series into trend-cycle, seasonal and remainder components to aid understanding and improve forecast accuracy.

nodes.label: Trend-Cycle Component
nodes.rationale: Trend and cycle combined into a single component, often just called 'the trend' for simplicity.

nodes.label: Remainder Component
nodes.rationale: What is left after trend-cycle and seasonal components are removed; contains anything else in the series.

nodes.label: Calendar Adjustment
nodes.rationale: Removes variation from differing numbers of trading days per month (e.g. average sales per trading day), simplifying patterns before analysis.

nodes.label: Population Adjustment
nodes.rationale: Convert totals to per-capita data so real changes are not confounded with population changes.

nodes.label: Inflation Adjustment
nodes.rationale: Restate financial series in constant dollars using a price index (e.g. CPI) so values across time are comparable.

nodes.label: Consumer Price Index (CPI)
nodes.rationale: A common price index for consumer goods used to adjust series for inflation.

nodes.label: Mathematical Transformation
nodes.rationale: Useful when variation increases/decreases with the level of the series; stabilises variance to make models simpler.

nodes.label: Logarithmic Transformation
nodes.rationale: Interpretable because changes in a log value are relative/percentage changes; not possible if any value is zero or negative.

nodes.label: Power Transformation
nodes.rationale: Transformations of the form w_t = y_t^p (e.g. square root, cube root); less interpretable than logs.

nodes.label: Box-Cox Transformation
nodes.rationale: A family parameterised by lambda that includes both logarithms (lambda=0) and power transformations; a good lambda makes seasonal variation roughly constant across the series.

nodes.label: Guerrero Method
nodes.rationale: An automatic feature for choosing the Box-Cox lambda parameter.

nodes.label: Additive Decomposition
nodes.rationale: y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t; appropriate when the magnitude of seasonal fluctuations does not vary with the level of the series.

nodes.label: Multiplicative Decomposition
nodes.rationale: y_t = S_t x T_t x R_t; appropriate when seasonal variation is proportional to the level; common with economic series. A log transform turns it into additive form.

nodes.label: Seasonally Adjusted Data
nodes.rationale: Series with the seasonal component removed (y_t - S_t additive, y_t / S_t multiplicative); highlights non-seasonal variation but is not smooth, so trend-cycle is better for spotting turning points.

nodes.label: Classical Decomposition
nodes.rationale: Method originating in the 1920s that uses moving averages to estimate the trend-cycle; basis of many later decomposition methods.

nodes.label: STL Decomposition
nodes.rationale: A robust decomposition method (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess) used to extract additive trend, seasonal and remainder components; allows the seasonal component to change over time.

nodes.label: Nagle & Müller — The Strategy and Tactics of Pricing
nodes.author: Thomas T. Nagle; Georg Müller
nodes.norm_label: nagle & muller — the strategy and tactics of pricing

nodes.label: Robert Phillips — Pricing and Revenue Optimization
nodes.author: Robert Phillips
nodes.norm_label: robert phillips — pricing and revenue optimization

nodes.label: Strategic Pricing
nodes.rationale: Proactively manage customer perceptions of value and shift demand curves rather than reacting to them; pricing becomes part of profitable growth strategy, not a blunt instrument to drive sales.

nodes.label: Value-Based Pricing
nodes.rationale: Set prices to capture a share of the economic value created for customers rather than to cover costs or hit sales goals.

nodes.label: Cost-Plus Pricing
nodes.rationale: Adds a markup to cost; criticized as a rigid, internally-focused process that ignores customer value and demand.

nodes.label: Customer-Driven Pricing
nodes.rationale: Lets customer reactions dictate price, risking surrender of pricing power to buyers.

nodes.label: Share-Driven Pricing
nodes.rationale: Prices set to win market share; the 'market-share myth' can destroy profitability.

nodes.label: Value Cascade
nodes.rationale: Six-stage framework (value creation, communication, structure, policy, level, competition) organizing strategic pricing tasks across both value and price.

nodes.label: Economic Value
nodes.rationale: The guiding force of pricing strategy: total monetary worth of an offer to a customer.

nodes.label: Economic Value Estimation (EVE)
nodes.rationale: Method estimating value as competitive reference price plus differentiation value (monetary + psychological).

nodes.label: Competitive Reference Price
nodes.rationale: The next-best competitive alternative's price, anchoring perceived value.

nodes.label: Monetary Value
nodes.rationale: Quantifiable financial benefit a product delivers vs. the reference alternative.

nodes.label: Psychological Value
nodes.rationale: Non-monetary, emotional, or perceptual worth influencing willingness-to-pay.

nodes.label: Willingness-to-Pay
nodes.rationale: Maximum price a customer will accept; strategic pricing aims to actively influence it, not just react to it.

nodes.label: Value-Based Market Segmentation
nodes.rationale: Six-step segmentation by discriminating value drivers and fences to align prices with value and cost.

nodes.label: Value Communication
nodes.rationale: Communicating value to shift willingness-to-pay; tailored by involvement level and benefit type.

nodes.label: Switching-Cost Effect
nodes.rationale: Buyers less price-sensitive when switching incurs cost.

nodes.label: Difficult-Comparison Effect
nodes.rationale: Harder cross-supplier comparison lowers price sensitivity.

nodes.label: Price-Quality Effect
nodes.rationale: Higher price signals higher quality, raising willingness-to-pay.

nodes.label: End-Benefit Effect
nodes.rationale: Price sensitivity tied to the share of total end-benefit cost.

nodes.label: Shared-Cost Effect
nodes.rationale: Buyers paying only part of cost are less price sensitive.

nodes.label: Fairness Effect
nodes.rationale: Perceived fairness of price relative to norms affects acceptance.

nodes.label: Price Structure
nodes.rationale: Tactics for pricing differently across customer segments via metrics, fences, and offer configurations.

nodes.label: Price Fences
nodes.rationale: Conditions (buyer ID, location, timing, quantity) that segment customers and prevent arbitrage across price tiers.

nodes.label: Price Metrics
nodes.rationale: The unit by which price is charged (per minute, per click, per use); aligning the metric with value can transform profitability.

nodes.label: Price Bundling
nodes.rationale: Selling combined offers to capture heterogeneous willingness-to-pay across components.

nodes.label: Unbundling
nodes.rationale: Strategically separating offers to charge for value individually.

nodes.label: Peak-Load Pricing
nodes.rationale: Higher prices during peak demand to manage capacity and capture value.

nodes.label: Yield Management
nodes.rationale: Adjusting prices over time to extract maximum profit from fixed perishable capacity (rooms, seats).

nodes.label: Pricing Policy
nodes.rationale: Consistent rules influencing customer expectations and purchase behavior; proactive policy beats reactive ad-hoc negotiation.

nodes.label: Policy-Based Price Negotiation
nodes.rationale: Proactive, rule-driven negotiation avoids the value-eroding cycle of reactive discounting.

nodes.label: Price Level / Price Setting
nodes.rationale: Six-step process to set prices capturing a share of value created, weighing data toward 'good' rather than perfectly optimal prices.

nodes.label: Viable / Reasonable Price Range
nodes.rationale: Bounded range between cost floor and value ceiling within which to set price.

nodes.label: Price Competition
nodes.rationale: Managing competitive conflict thoughtfully; react only after thinking to avoid provoking destructive responses.

nodes.label: Price War
nodes.rationale: Value-destroying spiral of mutual price cuts; the goal is to minimize the chance of provoking one.

nodes.label: Price Elasticity of Demand
nodes.rationale: Responsiveness of quantity demanded to price; central to breakeven and optimization.

nodes.label: Measurement of Price Sensitivity
nodes.rationale: Research techniques (panel data, experiments, surveys) supplementing managerial judgment.

nodes.label: Conjoint (Trade-Off) Analysis
nodes.rationale: Decomposes preferences across attributes including price to estimate value and willingness-to-pay.

nodes.label: Buy-Response Survey
nodes.rationale: Direct questioning to build purchase-probability curves across price points.

nodes.label: Incremental / Avoidable Cost
nodes.rationale: Only costs that change with the pricing decision are relevant; sunk and non-incremental fixed costs should not drive price.

nodes.label: Breakeven Sales Analysis
nodes.rationale: Volume change needed to keep profit constant after a price change, given contribution margin.

nodes.label: Contribution Margin
nodes.rationale: Price minus incremental cost; determines breakeven sensitivity to price changes.

nodes.label: Skim Pricing (Pricing an Innovation)
nodes.rationale: High initial price for innovations to harvest value from least price-sensitive early adopters.

nodes.label: Product Life-Cycle Pricing
nodes.rationale: Adapt pricing across inception, growth, maturity, and decline phases.

nodes.label: Transfer Pricing
nodes.rationale: Economically efficient internal prices between profit centers to avoid double-marginalization inefficiencies.

nodes.label: Export / Foreign-Currency Pricing
nodes.rationale: Generic strategies for managing exchange-rate price adjustments in foreign markets.

nodes.label: Strategic Pricing Capability
nodes.rationale: Talent, processes, data, decision rights, and incentives that embed value-based pricing into the organization.

nodes.label: Price Waterfall
nodes.rationale: Maps erosion from list price through discounts, rebates, and terms down to realized pocket price.

nodes.label: Pocket Price
nodes.rationale: Net price actually captured after all waterfall deductions; the true profitability lever.

nodes.label: Price Bands
nodes.rationale: Distribution of realized prices across customers, revealing dispersion and leakage.

nodes.label: Win-Loss Analysis
nodes.rationale: Analyzing won and lost deals to understand price-response and competitive position.

nodes.label: Sales Incentive Alignment
nodes.rationale: Designing incentives that reward profit, not just volume, to support pricing strategy.

nodes.label: Predatory Pricing
nodes.rationale: Pricing below cost to drive out competitors; legally constrained.

nodes.label: Price Fixing
nodes.rationale: Illegal horizontal or vertical collusion on price.

nodes.label: Price Discrimination
nodes.rationale: Charging different prices to different buyers; legally constrained but economically a form of differentiation.

nodes.label: Behavioral Pricing / Behavioral Economics
nodes.rationale: Applying behavioral-economics findings to manage price and value perceptions.

nodes.label: Price Management
nodes.rationale: End-to-end process: strategy, analysis, decision, implementation; 'pretium = price = value' is its core equation.

nodes.label: Price Strategy
nodes.rationale: Goals, value-price linkage, positioning, and shareholder-value orientation guiding price decisions.

nodes.label: Price and Shareholder Value
nodes.rationale: Price drives not only short-term profit but sustained increases in shareholder value.

nodes.label: Price Positioning
nodes.rationale: Choosing a price position (luxury, premium, medium, low, ultra-low) consistent with brand and value.

nodes.label: Luxury Price Position
nodes.rationale: Highest price tier exploiting prestige and exclusivity effects.

nodes.label: Premium Price Position
nodes.rationale: Above-market pricing justified by superior value and quality signal.

nodes.label: Low / Ultra-Low Price Position
nodes.rationale: Cost-leadership-enabled aggressive pricing to win volume and price-sensitive segments.

nodes.label: Price-Response Function
nodes.rationale: Relationship between price and quantity sold; foundation for elasticity and optimization.

nodes.label: Prestige (Snob/Veblen) Effect
nodes.rationale: Demand rising with price for status goods, inverting the normal price-response curve.

nodes.label: Giffen Paradox
nodes.rationale: Special case where demand rises as price rises for certain staple goods.

nodes.label: Neuro-Pricing
nodes.rationale: Using brain-research methods to understand price perception.

nodes.label: Psychological Pricing
nodes.rationale: Price points, anchoring, and threshold effects exploiting cognitive biases.

nodes.label: One-Dimensional Pricing
nodes.rationale: A single price per unit; spans rigid (cost/competition) to comprehensive optimization processes.

nodes.label: Competition-Oriented Pricing
nodes.rationale: Setting price by reference to competitors rather than cost or value.

nodes.label: Mathematical Price Optimization
nodes.rationale: Deriving profit-maximizing price from the price-response and cost functions (Amoroso-Robinson logic).

nodes.label: Price Optimization in Oligopoly
nodes.rationale: Incorporates competitor reaction hypotheses into the optimization.

nodes.label: Multidimensional Pricing
nodes.rationale: Multiple price parameters: differentiation, product-line, and bundling decisions.

nodes.label: Price Differentiation
nodes.rationale: Charging different prices across segments built on market segmentation; Simon's framing of price discrimination.

nodes.label: Product-Line Pricing
nodes.rationale: Coordinating prices across related products to account for substitution and complementarity.

nodes.label: Long-Term Price Optimization
nodes.rationale: Optimizing prices over time accounting for dynamic price-response, cost curves, and customer lifecycle.

nodes.label: Relationship / Customer-Lifetime Pricing
nodes.rationale: Price to maximize acquisition, retention, and win-back value over the full relationship, not single transactions.

nodes.label: Price and Inflation
nodes.rationale: Managing real price position under varying inflation rates.

nodes.label: International Price Management
nodes.rationale: Cross-border pricing, parallel imports, and government intervention.

nodes.label: Parallel Imports / Gray Market
nodes.rationale: Arbitrage across country price differences undermining international price differentiation.

nodes.label: Price Implementation
nodes.rationale: Organization, decision authority, sales-force role, communication, and controlling needed to realize prices.

nodes.label: Price Decision Authority
nodes.rationale: Allocating discounting/pricing rights between management and sales force.

nodes.label: Price Controlling
nodes.rationale: Monitoring tools and IT to track and steer realized prices.

nodes.label: Vertical Price Management
nodes.rationale: Coordinating manufacturer and retailer prices toward joint profit maximization and distribution of profit.

nodes.label: Price Image
nodes.rationale: Consumers' overall perception of a retailer's price level, shaped by signal items.

nodes.label: Price Promotions
nodes.rationale: Temporary price reductions to drive traffic and move merchandise.

nodes.label: Auction Pricing
nodes.rationale: Letting buyers reveal willingness-to-pay through competitive bidding.

nodes.label: Price Contracts and Hedging
nodes.rationale: Contracts that lock or hedge prices against input-cost volatility.

nodes.label: Flat-Rate Pricing
nodes.rationale: Single recurring charge for unlimited use; exploits flat-rate bias.

nodes.label: Freemium
nodes.rationale: Free base tier with paid premium upgrades to convert users.

nodes.label: Pay-Per-Use
nodes.rationale: Usage-based metric aligning price with consumption.

nodes.label: Pay-What-You-Want
nodes.rationale: Buyer sets the price; relies on fairness norms and reciprocity.

nodes.label: Two-Sided Price Systems
nodes.rationale: Platform pricing balancing two interdependent customer groups.

nodes.label: Negative Prices
nodes.rationale: Paying customers to consume, e.g. under zero marginal cost or grid-balancing conditions.

nodes.label: Price Transparency
nodes.rationale: Internet-driven visibility of prices intensifying competition and price wars; offset by value transparency.

nodes.label: Dynamic Pricing
nodes.rationale: Continuously adjusting prices to demand, competition, and conditions, enabled by data and algorithms.

nodes.label: Big Data & AI in Pricing
nodes.rationale: Machine learning and big data driving granular price-response estimation and optimization.

nodes.label: Pricing and Revenue Optimization
nodes.rationale: Discipline of trading off increased profit-per-transaction at higher prices against reduced penetration, across customer/product/channel.

nodes.label: Profit-Based Pricing
nodes.rationale: Maximizing profit by quantifying customer price-response per segment and setting prices that balance margin and volume.

nodes.label: Customer Price Sensitivity
nodes.rationale: Estimating segment-level price response is the key element risk-based pricing alone omits.

nodes.label: Risk-Based Pricing
nodes.rationale: Charging higher rates to higher-risk customers; sensible first step but ignores price sensitivity, falling short of best-practice profit-based pricing.

nodes.label: Loan / Consumer-Credit Pricing
nodes.rationale: Applying profit-based pricing to APRs across product/brand/channel/segment, subject to regulation.

nodes.label: Adverse Selection
nodes.rationale: Raising loan rates not only cuts volume but deteriorates the credit quality of accepting customers; unique to lending and must be modeled explicitly.

nodes.label: Lost-Quote / Win-Loss Data
nodes.rationale: Data on prospects who declined a price is valuable for predicting propensity to accept; banks largely waste it.

nodes.label: Price-Response Estimation
nodes.rationale: Statistical analysis of historical won/lost data to quantify which products are valued by which customers and by how much.

nodes.label: Regulatory Pricing Constraints
nodes.rationale: Pricing must be consistent, constrained, transparent, defensible, and not a black box (fair lending, Basel II).

nodes.label: Negotiated Pricing
nodes.rationale: In lending, optimized list rates must allow negotiation flexibility around indicated optimal rates.

nodes.label: Relationship / Cross-Product Pricing
nodes.rationale: Maximizing each product transaction may not be profit-optimal overall; price for cross-product and lifetime relationship value.

nodes.label: Gallego & Topaloglu: Revenue Management and Pricing Analytics
nodes.author: Guillermo Gallego; Huseyin Topaloglu
nodes.rationale: 2019 textbook organized in three parts: traditional RM (capacity control, overbooking), RM under customer choice (choice models, assortment optimization), and pricing analytics (pricing theory, dynamic pricing, online learning, competition).
nodes.norm_label: gallego & topaloglu: revenue management and pricing analytics

nodes.label: Talluri & van Ryzin: The Theory and Practice of Revenue Management
nodes.author: Kalyan Talluri; Garrett van Ryzin
nodes.rationale: 2004 foundational text synthesizing the RM field: single-resource and network capacity control, overbooking, dynamic pricing, customer behavior and choice modeling, economics, estimation/forecasting, industry context, and implementation.
nodes.norm_label: talluri & van ryzin: the theory and practice of revenue management

nodes.label: Revenue Management
nodes.rationale: Data-driven, computerized system to support tactical pricing of perishable assets at the micro-market level to maximize expected revenues from sunk investments in capacity.

nodes.label: Pricing Analytics
nodes.rationale: Goes beyond dynamic pricing to study consumer surplus and welfare, competition, and online learning.

nodes.label: Market Segmentation
nodes.rationale: Fare-class restrictions (time of sale, booking limits, advance purchase) segment willing-to-pay groups; profitable even in monopolies and with a single market segment.

nodes.label: Perishable Asset / Capacity
nodes.rationale: Fixed capacity that expires (airline seats, hotel rooms) so unsold units have zero or low salvage value, motivating capacity-controlled sale.

nodes.label: Fare Class
nodes.rationale: A menu of price, restrictions, and ancillary services targeting a segment; the basic unit of inventory control in RM.

nodes.label: Single-Resource Revenue Management
nodes.rationale: Allocate one fixed capacity (e.g., a flight leg cabin) across multiple fare classes; deciding how much to reserve for higher fares.

nodes.label: Independent Demand Model
nodes.rationale: Assumes demand for each fare class is independent and a denied customer leaves without buying; tractable but ignores recapture/substitution.

nodes.label: Littlewood's Rule
nodes.rationale: Two-class protection rule: protect units for the high fare while P{D1>=y} > fare ratio r=p2/p1; reject discount request when p1*P{D1>=y}>p2. Optimal protection level is independent of discount-fare demand distribution.

nodes.label: Protection Level
nodes.rationale: Units of capacity reserved for higher fare classes; complement of the booking limit for lower classes.

nodes.label: Booking Limit
nodes.rationale: Maximum capacity made available to a lower fare class; capacity minus protection level.

nodes.label: Capacity Allocation / Capacity Control
nodes.rationale: Quantity-based RM: decide how much capacity to make available to each fare class by opening/closing classes in response to remaining capacity.

nodes.label: Expected Marginal Seat Revenue (EMSR)
nodes.rationale: EMSR-a and EMSR-b heuristics set nested protection levels for multiple fare classes by equating marginal revenue across classes; the dominant industry single-resource heuristic.

nodes.label: Nesting (Theft vs Standard)
nodes.rationale: Higher fare classes can draw down capacity reserved against lower classes; theft vs standard nesting differ in how booking limits are decremented under different arrival patterns.

nodes.label: Fare Ratio
nodes.rationale: r=p2/p1 governs the protection level: lower r protects more for the full fare; near 1 accepts more discount demand.

nodes.label: Spill Rate
nodes.rationale: Probability of denying at least one high-fare customer; high spill rates risk strategic loss of full-fare customers to competitors.

nodes.label: Low-Before-High Arrival Pattern
nodes.rationale: Worst-case arrival order in which discount demand books before full-fare demand, making booking limits essential to protect high-fare inventory.

nodes.label: Callable Products
nodes.rationale: Discount sales that the seller can recall/buy back with compensation, improving spill rate and capacity utilization without sacrificing discount sales.

nodes.label: Generalized Newsvendor Problem
nodes.rationale: Setting initial capacity at increasing convex cost connects RM to the newsvendor critical-ratio logic.

nodes.label: Compound Poisson Demand
nodes.rationale: Arriving customers may request multiple units; outperforms sequential low-before-high models when that arrival pattern fails.

nodes.label: Revenue Opportunity Model
nodes.rationale: Benchmark bounding optimal expected revenue between accept-all and perfect-hindsight policies, used to measure heuristic quality.

nodes.label: Monotone / Non-Reopening Fare Policy
nodes.rationale: Policies that never reopen a closed fare class, deterring strategic customers from waiting for lower fares.

nodes.label: Network Revenue Management
nodes.rationale: Manages multi-leg itineraries that consume shared resources; an accepted itinerary may displace revenue on other legs.

nodes.label: Bid-Price Control
nodes.rationale: Accept a product if its fare exceeds the sum of bid prices (opportunity costs) of the resources it uses; a scalable network control.

nodes.label: Deterministic Linear Program (DLP)
nodes.rationale: Uses expected demand to allocate capacity; its dual prices give bid prices and an upper bound on the optimal value function.

nodes.label: Randomized Linear Program (RLP)
nodes.rationale: Averages bid prices over random demand samples to better capture stochasticity than the DLP.

nodes.label: Virtual Nesting
nodes.rationale: Maps network itineraries into a small number of virtual buckets per leg so single-resource nested control can be applied on a network.

nodes.label: Displacement Cost / Opportunity Cost
nodes.rationale: The marginal revenue foregone on other resources by accepting a request; the economic basis of bid prices.

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Decomposition
nodes.rationale: Decomposes the intractable network DP into single-resource DPs using DLP duals, fare allocation, or Lagrangian relaxation.

nodes.label: Lagrangian Relaxation
nodes.rationale: Relaxes coupling capacity constraints to decompose the network problem and produce tighter bounds than the DLP.

nodes.label: Approximate Dynamic Programming
nodes.rationale: Value-function approximation methods that scale network RM beyond exact DP.

nodes.label: Resolving Heuristic
nodes.rationale: Periodically re-solving the deterministic LP with updated remaining capacity and time to refresh bid prices/controls.

nodes.label: Probabilistic Admission Control
nodes.rationale: Accepts requests with a probability derived from the LP solution rather than deterministic open/close, smoothing capacity use.

nodes.label: Upgrades and Upsells
nodes.rationale: Policies to move customers to higher-value products, modeled as part of network formulations.

nodes.label: Overbooking
nodes.rationale: Selling more than physical capacity to offset cancellations and no-shows, trading the cost of denied service against spoilage.

nodes.label: No-Show
nodes.rationale: Booked customers who do not arrive at service time, the primary driver of overbooking.

nodes.label: Cancellation
nodes.rationale: Pre-departure withdrawals that free capacity, incorporated into overbooking and booking-limit models.

nodes.label: Denied Boarding / Overbooking Penalty
nodes.rationale: Cost of bumping a customer when realized demand exceeds capacity; the penalty side of the overbooking trade-off.

nodes.label: Book-and-Bump Strategy
nodes.rationale: Network overbooking heuristic that books beyond capacity then optimally bumps to minimize total penalty.

nodes.label: Choice-Based Revenue Management
nodes.rationale: Replaces independent-demand Littlewood logic with a DP over discrete choice models, capturing recapture and substitution.

nodes.label: Discrete Choice Model
nodes.rationale: Probabilistic model of customer selection from an offered set, foundation of choice-based RM and assortment optimization.

nodes.label: Random Utility Maximization Model
nodes.rationale: Customers pick the alternative with highest utility plus random noise; the theoretical basis for logit-family models.

nodes.label: Multinomial Logit (MNL) Model
nodes.rationale: Attraction-based choice model with closed-form choice probabilities; exhibits the Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives property.

nodes.label: Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives (IIA)
nodes.rationale: MNL property that the relative odds of two options are independent of other options; a key limitation motivating richer models.

nodes.label: Nested Logit Model
nodes.rationale: Groups alternatives into nests to relax IIA and capture correlated preferences within nests.

nodes.label: Generalized Attraction Model
nodes.rationale: Extends basic attraction/MNL to handle no-purchase shadow attractiveness and out-of-assortment effects.

nodes.label: Exponomial Choice Model
nodes.rationale: Choice model based on exponentially distributed utility shocks, an alternative to logit with different substitution patterns.

nodes.label: Random Consideration Set Model
nodes.rationale: Customers first form a random consideration set then choose within it, modeling limited attention.

nodes.label: Markov Chain Choice Model
nodes.rationale: Models substitution as Markovian transitions among products; approximates a broad class of choice models and yields tractable assortment/network results.

nodes.label: Mixtures of Attraction / Logit Models
nodes.rationale: Mixing basic attraction models over customer types adds heterogeneity but makes assortment optimization NP-hard.

nodes.label: Assortment Optimization
nodes.rationale: Choose the offer set maximizing expected revenue under a choice model; foundation of choice-based RM and retail/recommendation systems.

nodes.label: Constrained Assortment Optimization
nodes.rationale: Assortment problems with cardinality or display constraints, solved tractably under MNL and nested logit in special cases.

nodes.label: Efficient Sets / Revenue-Ordered Assortments
nodes.rationale: Convexity of the revenue-vs-probability frontier implies the optimal MNL assortment is revenue-ordered (nested by price).

nodes.label: Sales-Based Linear Program (SBLP)
nodes.rationale: Gallego-Topaloglu signature LP formulation of choice-based network RM in sales (purchase-probability) variables, avoiding enumeration of offer sets.

nodes.label: Column Generation
nodes.rationale: Solves the choice-based LP with exponentially many offer-set columns by generating only profitable ones via the assortment subproblem.

nodes.label: Demand-Intensity / Price Control
nodes.rationale: Treats price as the control that sets the demand (Poisson) intensity, the standard continuous-time dynamic pricing formulation.

nodes.label: Basic Pricing Theory
nodes.rationale: Firm's static pricing problem with linear costs, including existence and uniqueness conditions for optimal prices.

nodes.label: Representative Consumer Model
nodes.rationale: Derives demand from a representative consumer's utility maximization, dual to the firm's pricing problem.

nodes.label: Consumer Surplus and Welfare
nodes.rationale: Value to consumers beyond price paid; pricing analytics studies welfare and dead-weight loss effects of pricing policies.

nodes.label: Dead-Weight Loss / Social Planning
nodes.rationale: Welfare loss from pricing above marginal cost versus the social planner's allocation.

nodes.label: Peak-Load Pricing
nodes.rationale: Charging higher prices in high-demand periods to manage capacity utilization across time.

nodes.label: Priority Pricing
nodes.rationale: Offering differentiated service priority at differentiated prices to segment customers.

nodes.label: Pass-Through Rate
nodes.rationale: Fraction of a cost change passed to price; conditions on it determine when input randomness benefits both firm and consumer.

nodes.label: Benefit of Random Costs/Prices
nodes.rationale: Result that firms prefer random input costs and consumers prefer random prices under conditions on the pass-through rate.

nodes.label: Multi-Product Pricing
nodes.rationale: Joint pricing of substitutable products under linear, MNL, and nested logit demand.

nodes.label: Dynamic Nonlinear Pricing
nodes.rationale: Quantity-dependent price schedules evolving over the sales horizon.

nodes.label: Strategic Customers
nodes.rationale: Forward-looking customers who time purchases anticipating markdowns; motivate monotone pricing and non-reopening policies.

nodes.label: Fixed-Price Heuristic
nodes.rationale: A single static price for time-independent demand that is asymptotically optimal as volume scales.

nodes.label: Bid-Price Heuristic for Dynamic Pricing
nodes.rationale: Uses capacity shadow prices to set dynamic prices; shown asymptotically optimal as capacity and demand scale together.

nodes.label: Asymptotic Optimality
nodes.rationale: Heuristics whose revenue gap to optimal vanishes as the problem scales; the standard performance guarantee for RM heuristics.

nodes.label: Online Learning in Pricing
nodes.rationale: Simultaneously estimating demand and optimizing price/assortment during the sales horizon.

nodes.label: Learning-and-Earning (Explore-Exploit) Trade-off
nodes.rationale: Balancing price experimentation to learn demand against exploiting current estimates to earn revenue.

nodes.label: Regret
nodes.rationale: Performance metric measuring cumulative revenue loss versus a clairvoyant optimal policy in online learning.

nodes.label: Demand Learning / Estimation
nodes.rationale: Estimating demand parameters from historical sales for RM controls.

nodes.label: Unconstraining / Censored Demand
nodes.rationale: Recovering true demand from sales censored by closed fare classes/sold-out capacity, a core estimation-and-forecasting problem.

nodes.label: Demand Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Predicting future demand by class and time, feeding the optimization step of RM systems.

nodes.label: Demand Recapture / Substitution
nodes.rationale: When a closed class's demand is recaptured by open classes, violating the independent-demand assumption and motivating choice-based RM.

nodes.label: Buy-Up / Sell-Up Behavior
nodes.rationale: Customers shifting to a higher open fare when their preferred class is closed; modeled to refine protection levels.

nodes.label: Competitive Assortment Optimization
nodes.rationale: Firms simultaneously choosing assortments; analyzes existence and properties of Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label: Dynamic Pricing Under Competition
nodes.rationale: Oligopoly dynamic pricing with equilibrium analysis, comparative statics, and asymptotic optimality in the stochastic case.

nodes.label: Nash Equilibrium
nodes.rationale: Solution concept for competitive assortment and pricing games among RM firms.

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming
nodes.rationale: Stochastic DP over remaining capacity and time is the canonical exact formulation across single-resource, network, and choice-based RM.

nodes.label: Value Function Concavity / Monotonicity
nodes.rationale: Structural properties (concavity in capacity, monotone marginal value) that yield threshold/protection-level optimal policies.

nodes.label: Airline Deregulation Origin of RM
nodes.rationale: RM originated after late-1970s US airline deregulation; the American Airlines vs People Express super-saver fare saga is its founding story.

nodes.label: Chopra & Meindl: Supply Chain Management
nodes.author: Sunil Chopra; Peter Meindl
nodes.rationale: Textbook on supply chain strategy, planning, and operation.

nodes.label: Operations and Supply Chain Management
nodes.rationale: Textbook covering operations management and the supply chain function.

nodes.label: A Practical Price Optimization Approach for Omnichannel Retailing
nodes.author: Pavithra Harsha; Shivaram Subramanian; Markus Ettl (IBM Research)
nodes.rationale: INFORMS paper on integrated omnichannel demand estimation and price optimization.
nodes.norm_label: a practical price optimization approach for omnichannel retailing

nodes.label: Supply Chain Management
nodes.rationale: Management of flows of product, information, and funds across stages to maximize total supply chain surplus.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Surplus
nodes.rationale: Value generated = customer value minus total supply chain cost; the objective to maximize.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Decision Phases
nodes.rationale: Strategy/design, planning, and operation phases differ by time horizon and uncertainty.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Strategy
nodes.rationale: Long-term design of supply chain configuration and capabilities aligned to competitive strategy.

nodes.label: Strategic Fit
nodes.rationale: Consistency between competitive strategy and supply chain strategy; both target the same customer needs.

nodes.label: Implied Demand Uncertainty
nodes.rationale: Demand uncertainty a supply chain must handle given the customer needs it serves; drives required responsiveness.

nodes.label: Responsiveness vs. Efficiency
nodes.rationale: Core trade-off: ability to respond to demand variety/uncertainty vs. cost of producing/delivering; resolved via the cost-responsiveness efficient frontier.

nodes.label: Cost-Responsiveness Efficient Frontier
nodes.rationale: Curve of best cost achievable at each responsiveness level; firms position on it per strategy.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Drivers
nodes.rationale: Logistical and cross-functional drivers determining supply chain performance: facilities, inventory, transportation, information, sourcing, pricing.

nodes.label: Facilities
nodes.rationale: Physical locations (production, storage); decisions on role, location, capacity, flexibility.

nodes.label: Inventory
nodes.rationale: All raw materials, WIP, and finished goods; a key driver balancing responsiveness and cost.

nodes.label: Transportation
nodes.rationale: Movement of inventory between supply chain stages; mode and network design trade off cost vs. speed.

nodes.label: Information
nodes.rationale: Data and analysis connecting supply chain stages; the biggest lever for coordination and performance.

nodes.label: Sourcing
nodes.rationale: Choice of who performs supply chain activities: in-house vs. outsource, supplier selection.

nodes.label: Pricing
nodes.rationale: How a firm prices goods/services; shapes demand and matches it to supply.

nodes.label: Distribution Network Design
nodes.rationale: Design of how product moves from production to customer; trades off response time vs. inventory/transport cost.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Network Design
nodes.rationale: Facility location, capacity allocation, and market assignment; long-term strategic decisions.

nodes.label: Gravity Location Model
nodes.rationale: Method to find facility location minimizing transportation cost weighted by demand and supply points.

nodes.label: Outsourcing
nodes.rationale: Deciding to have third parties perform activities when they raise surplus more than in-house.

nodes.label: Supplier Selection
nodes.rationale: Evaluating and choosing suppliers on total cost of ownership, not just price.

nodes.label: Procurement
nodes.rationale: Acquiring goods and services from suppliers; part of the sourcing function.

nodes.label: Aggregate Planning
nodes.rationale: Planning production, inventory, and workforce levels over a medium horizon to balance supply and demand at minimum cost.

nodes.label: Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
nodes.rationale: Computing component requirements from the master schedule and bill of materials.

nodes.label: Scheduling
nodes.rationale: Allocating resources and timing operations to meet due dates and utilization goals.

nodes.label: Cycle Inventory
nodes.rationale: Average inventory due to ordering in lots larger than demand; trades ordering/holding cost.

nodes.label: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
nodes.rationale: Lot size minimizing the sum of fixed ordering cost and holding cost.

nodes.label: Quantity Discounts
nodes.rationale: Price breaks by volume; raise cycle inventory and can amplify the bullwhip effect.

nodes.label: Fill Rate
nodes.rationale: Fraction of demand satisfied from on-hand inventory; a product-level availability metric.

nodes.label: Reorder Point
nodes.rationale: Inventory level triggering a replenishment order in a continuous-review policy.

nodes.label: Continuous Review Policy
nodes.rationale: Inventory monitored continuously; order placed when stock hits reorder point.

nodes.label: Periodic Review Policy
nodes.rationale: Inventory reviewed at fixed intervals and ordered up to a target level.

nodes.label: Inventory Management
nodes.rationale: Policies and models governing how much and when to order/hold stock.

nodes.label: Risk Pooling / Aggregation
nodes.rationale: Aggregating demand across locations reduces variability, lowering required safety stock for the same service level.

nodes.label: Postponement
nodes.rationale: Delaying product differentiation until demand is known to exploit aggregation and cut safety stock.

nodes.label: Newsvendor Model
nodes.rationale: Single-period stocking decision under uncertainty balancing overage and underage cost.

nodes.label: Bullwhip Effect
nodes.rationale: Demand variability amplifies moving upstream, raising cost and reducing responsiveness.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Coordination
nodes.rationale: Aligning incentives and information across stages to act for total surplus; counters the bullwhip effect.

nodes.label: Double Marginalization
nodes.rationale: Each stage adding its own margin lowers total profit vs. a coordinated chain; motivates contracts.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Contracts
nodes.rationale: Buyback, revenue-sharing, and quantity-flexibility contracts realign incentives to coordinate the chain.

nodes.label: Push/Pull Processes
nodes.rationale: Push executes in anticipation of demand (forecast), pull in response to it; the boundary is a design choice.

nodes.label: Markdown Optimization
nodes.rationale: Timing and depth of price reductions to clear inventory while maximizing revenue.

nodes.label: Operations Management
nodes.rationale: Design and control of processes that transform inputs into goods and services.

nodes.label: Process Design
nodes.rationale: Configuring process type and flow to match volume-variety requirements.

nodes.label: Capacity Management
nodes.rationale: Matching available capacity to demand over time; key planning lever.

nodes.label: Bottleneck
nodes.rationale: Resource with the least capacity that limits overall system throughput.

nodes.label: Theory of Constraints
nodes.rationale: Manage the system by identifying and exploiting the constraint that governs throughput.

nodes.label: Quality Management
nodes.rationale: Methods to ensure outputs meet specifications and reduce variation.

nodes.label: Six Sigma
nodes.rationale: Data-driven DMAIC methodology to reduce defects and process variation.

nodes.label: Lean / Just-in-Time
nodes.rationale: Eliminating waste and producing in response to demand with minimal inventory.

nodes.label: Facility Layout
nodes.rationale: Physical arrangement of resources to optimize flow, distance, and utilization.

nodes.label: Project Management
nodes.rationale: Planning and controlling one-time efforts (CPM/PERT) under time and resource limits.

nodes.label: Productivity
nodes.rationale: Ratio of outputs to inputs; core operations performance metric.

nodes.label: Sustainability
nodes.rationale: Triple-bottom-line (economic, environmental, social) considerations in operations and supply chains.

nodes.label: Omnichannel Retailing
nodes.rationale: Seamless customer experience across brick-and-mortar and online channels; demands cross-channel coordination.

nodes.label: Omnichannel Pricing (OCP)
nodes.rationale: Profitably coordinating prices across channels and locations under volume and price constraints; projected 7% profit lift.

nodes.label: Omnichannel Fulfillment
nodes.rationale: Ship-from-store and buy-online-pickup-in-store options that span channels.

nodes.label: Buy-Online-Pickup-In-Store (BOPIS)
nodes.rationale: Fulfillment option letting customers buy online and collect in store.

nodes.label: Ship-From-Store
nodes.rationale: Fulfilling e-commerce orders from store inventory to enhance convenience and use distributed stock.

nodes.label: Showrooming
nodes.rationale: In-store shoppers comparing online prices on phones, eroding store sales; motivates cross-channel pricing.

nodes.label: Cross-Channel Substitution
nodes.rationale: Consumers switching between channels by price; must be captured by demand models to coordinate prices.

nodes.label: Independence from Irrelevant Alternatives (IIA)
nodes.rationale: MNL property forcing proportional substitution, giving unrealistic predictions; the nested model relaxes it.

nodes.label: Attraction Function
nodes.rationale: Function of price expressing channel/product attractiveness; basis of attraction choice models (MNL, MCI, linear).

nodes.label: Market Share Transformation
nodes.rationale: Reparameterizing prices as market shares to convexify pricing; insufficient under linear price constraints (Prop. 1).

nodes.label: Censored Demand Estimation
nodes.rationale: Calibrating demand when lost-sales data is unobserved; MIP loss-minimization imputes censored no-purchase choice.

nodes.label: Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP)
nodes.rationale: Discrete optimization used to solve the non-convex NP-hard OCP to (near) optimality with commercial solvers.

nodes.label: Reformulation-Linearization Technique (RLT)
nodes.rationale: Sherali-Adams linearization of bilinear terms; tighter relaxation gave ~57x runtime reduction in OCP.

nodes.label: Charnes-Cooper Fractional Transformation
nodes.rationale: Transforms ratio objective into linear form to remove non-linearity in the OCP MIP.

nodes.label: Non-Convex / NP-Hard Pricing
nodes.rationale: OCP with linear pricing constraints and multiple virtual channels is non-convex and NP-hard, ruling out simple gradient methods.

nodes.label: ML Demand Estimation Framework
nodes.rationale: Integrated data + ML pipeline estimating location-specific own/cross-channel elasticities at scale with LASSO and sign constraints.

nodes.label: Geographic Clustering / Virtual Stores
nodes.rationale: k-means partitioning of online demand into virtual stores reduces cross-channel dimensionality from O(|J|) to O(1).

nodes.label: Brand/Price Image Goals
nodes.rationale: Long-term constraints (price monotonicity, magic-number endings, volume goals) embedded as business rules in OCP.

nodes.label: Dynamic Pricing on E-commerce Platform with Deep RL: A Field Experiment
nodes.author: Jiaxi Liu, Yidong Zhang, Xiaoqing Wang, Yuming Deng, Xingyu Wu
nodes.rationale: First field experiment applying deep RL to dynamic pricing on a real E-commerce platform (Tmall.com).
nodes.norm_label: dynamic pricing on e-commerce platform with deep rl: a field experiment

nodes.label: An Exact Pricing Algorithm for Revenue Maximization under the Logit Demand Function
nodes.author: Moddassir Khan Nayeem, Omar Abbaas, Suzan Alaswad, Sinan Salman
nodes.rationale: Derives a closed-form revenue-maximizing price under logit demand using the Lambert W function.
nodes.norm_label: an exact pricing algorithm for revenue maximization under the logit demand function

nodes.label: Multi-Agent RL for Dynamic Pricing in Supply Chains
nodes.author: Thomas Hazenberg, Yao Ma, Seyed Sahand Mohammadi Ziabari, Marijn van Rijswijk
nodes.rationale: Benchmarks MADDPG, MADQN, and QMIX against rule-based pricing baselines in a simulated supply chain market.
nodes.norm_label: multi-agent rl for dynamic pricing in supply chains

nodes.label: No-Regret Learning for Stackelberg Equilibrium in Newsvendor Pricing Games
nodes.author: Larkin Liu, Yuming Rong
nodes.rationale: Applies online learning and contextual bandits to compute Stackelberg equilibrium in a supplier-retailer newsvendor pricing game.
nodes.norm_label: no-regret learning for stackelberg equilibrium in newsvendor pricing games

nodes.label: Revenue Maximization
nodes.rationale: Choosing the price that maximizes revenue R=p*d(p), the optimization objective in exact and Stackelberg pricing.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning
nodes.rationale: Learning a policy through reward-based interaction with an environment; applied to pricing in three of the four papers.

nodes.label: Deep Reinforcement Learning
nodes.rationale: RL with deep neural network function approximation for high-dimensional state spaces.

nodes.label: Markov Decision Process
nodes.rationale: Formalism modeling pricing as states, actions, rewards, and transitions; the basis for RL-based pricing.

nodes.label: Q-Learning
nodes.rationale: Value-iteration method computing optimal Q-values; used as a discrete-action pricing solver.

nodes.label: Deep Q-Network (DQN)
nodes.rationale: Neural-network approximation of Q-values enabling discrete pricing over high-dimensional states.

nodes.label: Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)
nodes.rationale: Actor-critic algorithm producing continuous pricing actions; outperformed DQN in field experiments.

nodes.label: Actor-Critic Method
nodes.rationale: Combines value-iteration (critic) and policy-iteration (actor) methods; underlies DDPG and A2C.

nodes.label: Policy Gradient
nodes.rationale: Adjusts policy-network parameters in the direction of the performance gradient.

nodes.label: Continuous Action Space
nodes.rationale: Extending pricing from a discrete price set to exact continuous prices; a key contribution of the deep RL pricing paper.

nodes.label: Reward Function
nodes.rationale: Signal driving RL learning; the paper shows reward design critically affects convergence in pricing.

nodes.label: Difference of Revenue Conversion Rates (DRCR)
nodes.rationale: Novel reward function (revenue/UV difference over time) that solves the convergence problem for FMCGs where raw revenue fails.

nodes.label: Revenue Conversion Rate (RCR)
nodes.rationale: Revenue divided by unique visitors; more concave than raw revenue and a better pricing reward base.

nodes.label: Cold Start Problem
nodes.rationale: Poor initial RL performance risking capital loss; addressed by pre-training on historical data.

nodes.label: Learning from Demonstration (DQfD/DDPGfD)
nodes.rationale: Pre-training RL agents from historical specialist pricing decisions to mitigate cold start.

nodes.label: Experience Replay
nodes.rationale: Replay buffer of transitions decorrelating updates for stable deep RL training.

nodes.label: Markdown Pricing
nodes.rationale: Limited-supply pricing application where the episode ends at stock-out; one of two studied scenarios.

nodes.label: Difference-in-Differences (DID)
nodes.rationale: Causal evaluation technique used with simi-products to assess pricing policies when A/B testing is illegal.

nodes.label: Field Experiment
nodes.rationale: Real-world online deployment pricing thousands of SKUs on Tmall.com over months.

nodes.label: Logit Demand Function
nodes.rationale: Bounded S-shaped demand curve from discrete choice theory; more realistic than linear/power/exponential demand.

nodes.label: Demand Function
nodes.rationale: Mathematical relationship between price and demand; the foundation of all pricing optimization here.

nodes.label: Lambert W Function
nodes.rationale: Special function solving transcendental equations z*e^z=x; yields the closed-form optimal price under logit demand.

nodes.label: Exact Pricing Algorithm (EPA)
nodes.rationale: Closed-form algorithm deriving the global revenue-maximizing price under logit demand via the Lambert W function.

nodes.label: Inflection Point Price
nodes.rationale: Price of steepest demand slope, often misused as market price; proven to overestimate the true revenue-maximizing price.

nodes.label: Closed-Form Solution
nodes.rationale: Analytical solution avoiding heuristic iterative root-finding; the central contribution of the EPA paper.

nodes.label: Unimodality of Revenue Function
nodes.rationale: The revenue function is proven strictly increasing then decreasing, guaranteeing a unique global maximizer.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL)
nodes.rationale: Multiple interacting agents learning pricing strategies, capturing supply-chain interdependence that single-agent RL ignores.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent DDPG (MADDPG)
nodes.rationale: Extends DDPG to multi-agent settings with centralized critics; balanced, stable pricing in experiments.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent DQN (MADQN)
nodes.rationale: Decentralized DQN with discrete pricing actions; most aggressive, highest revenue but lowest fairness and most volatile.

nodes.label: QMIX
nodes.rationale: Value-based MARL using a monotonic mixing network for coordinated joint action-values; strong in cooperative settings.

nodes.label: Centralized Training Decentralized Execution (CTDE)
nodes.rationale: Agents share information during training but act independently at execution; improves MARL learning stability.

nodes.label: LightGBM Demand Model
nodes.rationale: Gradient-boosting model forecasting demand (test R^2=0.74), forming the backbone of the market simulation.

nodes.label: Simulation Environment
nodes.rationale: Weekly time-stepped competitive market where agents set prices and observe demand feedback.

nodes.label: Rule-Based Pricing
nodes.rationale: Static cost-plus/competitor-matching strategies; high fairness and stability but no competitive adaptation.

nodes.label: Jain's Fairness Index
nodes.rationale: Metric quantifying market-share equity among agents; rule-based agents reached 0.9896, MADQN only 0.5844.

nodes.label: Price Volatility
nodes.rationale: Stability metric; aggressive MARL agents trade higher volatility for revenue, rule-based agents stay most stable.

nodes.label: ERP System
nodes.rationale: Enterprise resource planning systems that traditionally rely on static rule-based pricing; the deployment target for MARL pricing.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Optimization
nodes.rationale: Optimizing interdependent decisions across supply chain actors; the application domain of MARL and Stackelberg pricing.

nodes.label: Stackelberg Game
nodes.rationale: Leader-follower sequential game; supplier sets wholesale price first, retailer best-responds.

nodes.label: Stackelberg Equilibrium
nodes.rationale: Joint leader-follower strategy proven unique under perfect information in the newsvendor pricing game.

nodes.label: Price-Setting Newsvendor
nodes.rationale: Newsvendor that also chooses retail price; optimal price differs from the riskless price by a supply-shortage term.

nodes.label: Contextual Linear Bandit
nodes.rationale: Bandit algorithm with linear reward and confidence sets; used to learn demand parameters in the Stackelberg game.

nodes.label: Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty (OFUL)
nodes.rationale: Confidence-ball bandit algorithm; the follower best-responds optimistically using upper-bound reward estimates.
nodes.norm_label: optimism in the face of uncertainty (oful)

nodes.label: No-Regret Learning
nodes.rationale: Learning with sublinear cumulative regret; the algorithmic goal for both leader and follower.

nodes.label: Stackelberg Regret
nodes.rationale: Leader's cumulative reward gap versus optimal commitment; the quantity the proposed algorithm bounds.

nodes.label: Best Response Function
nodes.rationale: Follower's optimal action given the leader's; proven unique and monotonic in the newsvendor game.

nodes.label: Critical Fractile
nodes.rationale: Ratio (p-a)/p giving the optimal newsvendor order quantity via the inverse demand CDF.

nodes.label: Riskless Price
nodes.rationale: Profit-maximizing price ignoring inventory risk; an upper bound on the price-setting newsvendor's optimal price.

nodes.label: Why DRCR over raw revenue
nodes.rationale: On E-commerce, customer traffic fluctuates and dominates revenue, so raw revenue is non-concave and non-convergent; the difference of revenue conversion rates gives a stable, concave reward that converges even for FMCGs.

nodes.label: Why optimal price lies below inflection point
nodes.rationale: Since R'(p_inf)<0 when alpha<-2 and elasticity decreases monotonically reaching -1 before the inflection point, the revenue-maximizing price is strictly to the left, giving ~20% lower price and ~15% higher revenue.
nodes.norm_label: why optimal price lies below inflection point

nodes.label: Why MARL over single-agent RL
nodes.rationale: Single-agent RL assumes a stationary environment and ignores that a retailer's price change affects supplier production and inventory; MARL models the interdependent strategic interactions of multiple supply chain actors.

nodes.label: Why use an upper-bounding approximation
nodes.rationale: The true expected newsvendor profit requires inverse-CDF lookups and integral equations with no closed form; the concave riskless-profit upper bound UB(p|a)=(theta0-theta1*p)(p-a) is parametric and aligns with optimistic bandit learning.
